translation.content: "Combining sounds that wouldn't normally be thought of together." So reads the final line of Mexican DJ and producer 1OO1O's short-but-sweet artist bio. It's a claim many make but few pull off. Are truly new fusions still even possible in 2025? Hasn't electronic music reached saturation point? Not in the hands of 1OO1O, AKA uno cero cero uno cero, or one O O one O. For the best part of a decade, the Xalapa-born artist has been mashing up footwork, jungle, techno, electro, UK garage and breaks with Latin America's myriad folkloric styles, from salsa and merengue to mambo and dembow. The results, like 2023's SALSA & BREAX, or last year's SALÓN CANDELA, feel electrifyingly fresh, connecting threads across eras and traditions with a subtle dexterity. The secret is a deep respect for the source material. Indeed, foregrounding the best of Latin America—and specifically Mexico—is the dominant theme on RA.1010 (do you like what we did there?). By his own admission, 1OO1O switched up his DJing a couple years ago to prioritise music made locally or regionally by himself and his friends. At a time when Latin American culture is both at its peak and yet still constantly "reshaping itself to accommodate tourists and 'expats,'" his sets in clubs, festivals and radio stations around the world are his way of spreading the gospel. This 70-minute mix, then, is heavy on both Mexican talent—Regal86, DNZA, AAAA, Loris, FRAN G—and unreleased 1OO1O tracks. It's tough, rhythmic and strikingly modern, with just the right balance of light and intensity. Given the glut of local music, a late cameo from London grime MC Trim glistens like an emerald in the sun. If you want to know what the cool kids are raving to in Monterrey, Guadalajara and Mexico City right now, here's your answer. - Carlos Hawthorn What have you been up to recently? Oof, working a lot. Just touched down from my first-ever Toronto show at Veneno—easily one of the top five gigs I've ever played—and now gearing up for a few US dates. Trying keeping a healthy routine balancing work, music and movement. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? I recorded this mix at my home and studio in Mexico City, using three CDJ-2000 NXS players and a DJM-900 NXS mixer. A couple years ago I used to really dig deep for fresh and obscure tracks, but ever since I committed to making music my DJing has turned those rare finds into unreleased tracks made by myself and my best friends. Sometimes that makes me feel pretty distant from the DJ ecosystem, buried deep in the production world. This mix reflects just that, showcasing a lot of new music from myself and friends like Loris, Regal86, AAAA, Andy Martin, FRAN G, DNZA, Les Vins, Brenda, WOST, Skiis, Brick, Zero Idea, Trax Unit and Andrew Bon Bosher. You'll hear different shades of techno: electro, trance, hard drum, tribal techno, hardgroove and speed guaracha. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Releasing more music. I'm really excited for upcoming releases on Club Romántico, TraTraTrax, Wavawava, Capp Street Project, Nervous and of course self-releases, too. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The rise of fascism around the world feels impossible to ignore at this point. It seems like the vast accessibility to information that once offered the hope for collective intelligence has instead become a weapon for plain ignorance and intolerance, allowing fascist views to infiltrate not only governments but also our everyday lives. On the one hand, we see manhunt policies carried out by global north countries. On the other, here in Latin America, particularly in Mexico, we witness our culture reshaping itself to accommodate tourists and "expats": our gastronomy losing its spice, our neighbours losing their homes to rent hikes, and narco and sex tourism on the rise—all happening while Latin American culture is at its peak. One can only seek refuge in compassion and creation. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Back in 2017 and 2018, Yu Yu Cine Club was really important for me to understand the boundaries and possibilities of club music. I used to be almost exclusively into footwork and jungle, and attending Yu Yu allowed me to experience the sounds of electro, acid, techno, trance and house in a proper club environment. And for a party, Lapi has been definitive for my production style. Cami and the Lapi team have procured and cultivated a crowd that listens, dances and sweats. Some of my funnest music was made specifically for Lapi.
translation.blurb: The pulsating sound of Mexico in 2025.
